articles.author: Eric Tucker, Alanna Durkin Richer
articles.description: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche says the accused gunman who tried to breach the ballroom at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner is believed to have been targeting members of the Trump administration. Blanche says officials believe the suspect traveled by train from California to Chicago and then on to Washington, where he checked in as a guest to the hotel where one of Washington’s glitziest events was being held Saturday night. President Donald Trump was rushed off the stage at the sound of gunshots. The 31-year-old suspect is in custody and facing charges. Blanche was interviewed on NBC’s “Meet the Press.”
articles.title: Attacker at correspondents' dinner was said to be targeting top officials

articles.author: Meg Kinnard
articles.description: The Justice Department is pushing to dismiss a lawsuit blocking President Donald Trump's $400 million White House ballroom project. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche cites a shooting at the White House Correspondents' Dinner as a security concern. Assistant Attorney General Brett Shumate has given the National Trust for Historic Preservation until Monday to drop the suit. The group sued after the East Wing was demolished for the ballroom. Trump claims the project is privately funded, but public money is used for security upgrades. Some politicians, including Democrats, support the project, citing security needs.
articles.title: Justice Department urges end to lawsuit over Trump's $400M ballroom

articles.author: Will Weissert
articles.description: President Donald Trump is calling for unity after what he says he feels was a third assassination attempt. He spoke on Saturday night about the experience shortly after a man armed with guns and knives tried to rush past the security perimeter inside a Washington hotel where the president was about to speak to the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner. Trump suggested his politics made him a target. The Republican president is emphasizing the need for bipartisan healing. And he's comparing himself to Abraham Lincoln, saying that significant political figures are often targeted.
articles.title: Trump again calls for unity. But for how long?

articles.author: Michael Biesecker, Michael Kunzelman
articles.description: Social media posts that appear to match the California man arrested in the shooting at the White House correspondents’ dinner show he is a highly educated tutor and amateur video game developer. A May 2025 profile photo of Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance appears to match the appearance of the man in a photo of the alleged attacker being taken into custody that was posted Saturday night by President Donald Trump. According to his online resume, the 31-year-old has degrees in mechanical engineering and computer science. For the last six years he worked for a company that offers admissions counseling and test preparation services to aspiring college students.
articles.title: Accused correspondents’ dinner attacker is tutor from California

articles.author: David Bauder
articles.description: More often than not, big stories happen out of the sight of journalists. Not so this weekend, when a shooting forced the postponement of the White House Correspondents' Association dinner — an annual event attended by hundreds of the nation's most prominent reporters and editors. Once it became apparent that something serious had happened, many of the journalists in tuxedos and gowns moved swiftly into work mode — recording video and photos of the scene on their mobile phones, and wrestling with spotty service to get the news out as swiftly as they could. Fear swept the Washington Hilton ballroom, but many reporters set it aside to work.
articles.title: Washington's journalists reported as a big story came right to them

articles.author: Calvin Woodward
articles.description: The White House Correspondents’ Association dinner is one of Washington’s curious rituals. Once a year the president, the press and all the president’s men and women assemble in a hotel ballroom. They drink and dine as if friends. They crack jokes. They dress in formal wear. That ritual was upended Saturday night when a gunman charged the premises and tried to penetrate the ballroom where President Donald Trump and about 2,300 people had gathered. Trump was spirited out unharmed and the crowd hunkered down. Confusion reigned. There were gasps and broken plates. Lots of wine was spilled.
articles.title: Inside the ballroom where fear intruded on Trump and the press

articles.description: First-hand account of Trump's evacuation after security incident at correspondents' dinner
articles.title: First-hand account of Trump's evacuation after security incident at correspondents' dinner

articles.description: Investigators have not publicly named the suspect, but two law enforcement officials familiar with the matter have identified him to The Associated Press as 31-year-old Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California.
articles.title: What to know about the suspect in the White House Correspondents' dinner incident

articles.description: Security video appears to show suspect running towards White House correspondents' dinner
articles.title: Security video appears to show suspect running towards White House correspondents' dinner

articles.description: President Donald Trump struck a somber tone as he addressed reporters at the White House, saying being president is “a dangerous profession” and that attempted violence against him is “part of the job.”
articles.title: Trump says being president is a ‘dangerous profession’ after correspondents' dinner shooting
